title: Pornography
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Pornography
summary: Though Scripture predates modern pornography, its teachings on lust, the dignity of the human body, and the renewal of the mind speak directly to a struggle that enslaves millions today.
body:
The word pornography does not appear in Scripture, but the reality it addresses — consuming sexual imagery for arousal outside the covenant of marriage — falls squarely within the biblical category of lust. Jesus identifies the problem at its root: looking at another person with lustful intent is already a violation of the heart [verse:matt.5.28].

Pornography reduces image-bearers of God to objects of consumption. Genesis establishes that every human being carries the imago Dei [verse:gen.1.27]. To use a person — even a digital image of a person — as a means of self-gratification is to degrade what God has dignified. This is not merely a rule violation; it is a failure to see rightly.

Paul offers a path forward rooted in mental renewal. He urges believers not to conform to the patterns of this world but to be transformed by the renewing of their minds [verse:rom.12.2]. The battle against pornography is, at its core, a battle for what the mind dwells on. He elaborates in Philippians: fix your thoughts on what is true, honorable, pure, and admirable [verse:phil.4.8].

The struggle is real and often deeply entrenched. But Job's ancient covenant with his eyes [verse:job.31.1] and the psalmist's resolve to set nothing worthless before his gaze [verse:ps.101.3] show that believers have always had to fight for purity of sight. Freedom requires honesty, accountability, and the daily choice to bring the mind under the Spirit's governance.

verses.annotation: Jesus identifies lustful looking as adultery of the heart, directly addressing the act of consuming sexual imagery.
Every person bears God's image — pornography degrades what God has dignified.
Transformation comes through renewing the mind, not merely suppressing behavior.
Paul's call to fill the mind with what is pure and honorable as a counter to destructive thought patterns.
Job's covenant with his eyes — a proactive commitment to guard what he looked at.
The psalmist's resolve to set nothing worthless before his eyes.
The lust of the eyes as part of worldly desire that is passing away.

scholars.quote: Keener argues that Jesus' teaching on lust in the Sermon on the Mount anticipated cultures saturated with sexual imagery, grounding purity in the orientation of the heart rather than mere behavioral avoidance.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Moo reads Romans 12:2 as the hinge of Paul's ethics — transformation of the mind is not optional but the mechanism by which believers discern and desire what is good, making it directly applicable to habitual sin patterns.
Clines notes that Job's covenant with his eyes in chapter 31 represents the highest standard of sexual ethics in the ancient world, a voluntary self-limitation rooted in respect for others' dignity.
